skill.name: claude-code-headless
skill.description: Run Claude Code in headless mode for scripts, CI pipelines, structured output, session reuse, and controlled tool access.
skill.summary: Automate Claude Code CLI workflows without the interactive interface.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command, environment, filesystem, and network patterns. Most high-volume detections are false positives from Markdown code fences, CLI flag tables, and official documentation links, but the skill does teach headless command execution with broad tools and CI secrets. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, so publication is acceptable with security warnings.

medium_findings.title: Headless Claude Code Commands With Broad Tool Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill documents non-interactive Claude Code commands and examples that allow Bash, Read, Write, WebSearch, and auto-accepted edits. This is legitimate for automation, but unsafe prompts or untrusted repositories could cause file changes, command execution, or data exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly enable headless execution with powerful tools. The risk depends on operator configuration and prompt trust, so this is a confirmed contextual risk rather than malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Permission Bypass Mode Is Documented Without Guardrails
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill describes bypassPermissions as a mode that skips all prompts. In headless automation this can remove the last approval layer before tool use, especially when prompts include repository or CI data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly describe bypassing prompts. No example uses bypassPermissions, which lowers severity, but publishing should warn users about this mode.

medium_findings.title: Web Service Examples Invoke Claude Code From User Requests
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The Express and FastAPI examples accept request prompts and optional tool lists before spawning Claude Code. The examples use argument arrays, which limits shell injection, but they omit authentication, rate limits, prompt validation, and tool allowlists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The spawn and subprocess calls are real and receive request-derived values. The code avoids shell interpolation, so the main concern is exposed automation control rather than direct command injection.

medium_findings.title: CI Examples Handle Repository Data And Secret Variables
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The CI examples combine pull request diffs, Claude Code execution, Git platform tokens, and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. This is expected for CI automation, but users must avoid sending secrets or untrusted instructions to broad tool configurations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret references are standard CI patterns and are not exfiltrated in the examples. Risk remains because untrusted PR content is fed into an automated agent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Many external command detections come from Markdown inline code, fenced shell examples, and CLI flag names. They are documentation examples, not executable skill code loaded by the marketplace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code blocks and tables. There is no script file or runtime hook that executes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are Table Header False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported weak cryptography lines match words such as description or metadata, not cryptographic API usage. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA1 hashing, or encryption logic in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation text at the flagged lines, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched substrings inside ordinary words.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation Links Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs point to official Claude Code documentation. They do not collect data, execute code, or direct traffic to an unknown endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are plain reference links. No fetch, curl execution, or telemetry code is present at those lines.

low_findings.title: Device File Access Is Shell Redirection Noise
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The filesystem detections at stderr redirection are normal shell examples using /dev/null. They do not read sensitive files or traverse user directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path is used only to suppress command errors. It is a standard shell redirection pattern with no exfiltration behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-Accepted Edits In CI
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples show acceptEdits with Write or file generation tools. Automated edits should be limited to trusted repositories and reviewed before merge.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and can be safe in controlled CI. It becomes risky when prompts or repositories are untrusted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Request-Controlled Tool Selection
dangerous_patterns.description: The web integration examples let callers influence the Claude Code prompt and tool list. Production services should enforce server-side tool allowlists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly read prompt and tools from request objects. The risk is mitigated by argv arrays but remains at the application control layer.

dangerous_patterns.title: Untrusted Diff Review With Web Search Enabled
dangerous_patterns.description: Some examples send repository content or pull request diffs to Claude Code while allowing WebSearch. Users should avoid network tools for sensitive code reviews unless policy permits it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands combine code review inputs with network-capable tools. The files do not show direct exfiltration, so this is a policy and configuration risk.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable Claude Code runs without an interactive terminal. This skill explains headless CLI usage, structured output, CI patterns, and tool controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code headless mode with the --print flag.
Shows text, JSON, and stream-json output formats for automation.
Documents session resume and continuation patterns for multi-step tasks.
Provides CI examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins.
Shows Node.js, Python, and shell integration patterns.
Describes allowed and disallowed tool configuration for safer runs.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install Claude Code.
Examples require a configured Claude Code CLI and valid API credentials.
Security controls depend on the user choosing safe tools and prompts.
Some web and CI examples need production hardening before use.

use_cases.title: Automated Pull Request Review
use_cases.description: Run Claude Code against pull request diffs and publish structured review summaries in CI.

use_cases.title: Scripted Codebase Analysis
use_cases.description: Build shell workflows that analyze files, capture JSON output, and continue sessions across steps.

use_cases.title: Claude Code Service Integration
use_cases.description: Prototype Node.js or Python services that call Claude Code and stream results to another system.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Read-Only Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner use in a local repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the safest Claude Code headless command to analyze this repository without editing files or running shell commands.

prompt_templates.title: Create A JSON Automation Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a headless Claude Code script that returns JSON, checks for errors, and saves the final result to a report file.

prompt_templates.title: Build A CI Review Step
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI review pattern for Claude Code that reviews a pull request diff with restricted tools and avoids exposing secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Harden A Web Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review a web service design that calls Claude Code headlessly. Add authentication, rate limits, prompt validation, and a strict tool allowlist.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a safe command to inspect a codebase in headless mode.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a read-only Claude Code command, explains each flag, and warns against write or shell tools.

output_examples.input: A CI engineer needs structured results for a pull request review.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a pipeline step that captures Claude Code output, checks for errors, and stores a markdown summary.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants to expose Claude Code through an internal service.
output_examples.output: The skill describes process spawning patterns and highlights required safeguards for prompts, tools, and access control.

best_practices: Default to read-only tools for analysis tasks and add write tools only when required.
Keep API keys and Git tokens in CI secret stores, never in prompts or logs.
Use JSON or stream-json output when another system must parse results.
anti_patterns: Do not use bypassPermissions for untrusted repositories or user-submitted prompts.
Do not let external callers choose arbitrary Claude Code tools in a web service.
Do not combine sensitive source code with network tools unless policy allows it.

faq.question: What does this skill help me automate?
faq.answer: It helps you run Claude Code from scripts, CI jobs, and services without using the interactive interface.

faq.question: Does it install Claude Code for me?
faq.answer: No. It assumes Claude Code is already installed and configured in the target environment.

faq.question: Which output formats are covered?
faq.answer: It covers plain text, JSON, and stream-json output for different automation needs.

faq.question: Can I use it in CI pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, scheduled jobs, and pre-commit hooks.

faq.question: Is headless mode safe for production automation?
faq.answer: It can be safe when tools are restricted, prompts are trusted, secrets are protected, and outputs are reviewed.

faq.question: Why does the audit warn about this skill?
faq.answer: The skill teaches powerful automation patterns. Some examples need strict permissions, authentication, and secret handling.
